head_coach.name: Joe Brady
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Sean Payton coaching tree; spread concepts with West Coast roots; emphasizes spacing, tempo, RPOs, and getting playmakers the ball in space; philosophy of forcing defenses to defend every blade of grass

offensive_coordinator.name: Pete Carmichael Jr.
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Sean Payton / West Coast / Erhardt-Perkins hybrid

defensive_coordinator.name: Jim Leonhard
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Rex Ryan 3-4 / Dave Aranda simulated pressure / Vance Joseph (Wade Phillips lineage)

offense.scheme_family: Sean Payton tree spread / West Coast hybrid
offense.run_scheme: inside zone / gap with increasing play-action and under-center looks via Carmichael influence
offense.pass_concepts: spacing concepts, shallow crosses, stick routes, RPOs, play-action bootlegs; quick game emphasis with horizontal stretch; vertical shots off play action; RB screen game expected to expand under Carmichael/Payton system
offense.te_usage: move TE / flex weapon; Kincaid lines up in slot, inline, and flexed out as de facto WR4; 29% of 2025 snaps featured 2+ TEs; Carmichael/Payton system historically employs heavier personnel with under-center play action, suggesting continued diverse TE usage
offense.rb_usage: bell cow runner (Cook led NFL in rushing with 1,621 yards in 2025) but historically underused as receiver (only 33 catches/291 yards); Carmichael/Payton system values RB screen game and receiving backs, so expect expanded receiving role in 2026

defense.alignment: odd front, 3 down linemen with 2 stand-up OLBs; transitioning from McDermott's 4-3 to Leonhard's 3-4; separate ILB and OLB coaches hired to reflect scheme change
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up primary, with simulated pressures frequently sending ILBs and dropping DEs; Leonhard's creeper/sim pressure package disguises the actual pass rusher, creating 4-man rush looks from non-traditional rushers
defense.coverage_base: MOFC (middle of field closed) heavy — Cover 1 and Cover 3 base with pattern matching; heavy use of coverage disguises; clogs middle of field with LBs/safeties and funnels receivers inside
defense.blitz_tendency: heavy — Leonhard learned under Vance Joseph in Denver where Broncos blitzed at 4th-highest rate (35.9%); however many pressures are simulated (4-man rush with deceptive personnel), not true Cover 0 blitzes; expects significant increase from Buffalo's prior 22.9% blitz rate
defense.lb_role: OLBs are primary edge rushers with coverage versatility; ILBs handle run fits and coverage responsibilities; simulated pressures frequently involve LBs creeping to rush while DEs drop into coverage zones
defense.safety_usage: Leonhard, a former safety, emphasizes safety play as a defensive cornerstone; expects aggressive ball-hawking safeties; Cole Bishop projects as a centerpiece; strong safety likely plays near box in run support with coverage flexibility

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Buffalo's offense under Brady treats the TE as a true receiving weapon. Dalton Kincaid lined up in 16 different spots including heavy slot usage (3rd among TEs in slot snaps), functioning as a de facto WR. Kincaid posted elite receiving metrics (PFF receiving grade of 81.9, 2.7 yards per route run — 1st among TEs) in 2025. The Payton/Carmichael system historically features TEs as primary pass catchers (Jimmy Graham, Taysom Hill archetypes). Brady's 'Everybody Eats' philosophy and spread formations create massive value for any TE with route running, hands, and separation ability. A receiving TE is valued like a WR3/WR4 in this offense.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Leonhard's switch to a 3-4 base with separate OLB/ILB coaches fundamentally changes how Buffalo values LBs. OLBs are now the primary edge rushers — Greg Rousseau may transition to a two-point stance in this scheme. Leonhard's system from Wisconsin heavily used simulated pressures where LBs creep to rush and DEs drop, meaning any LB with pass rush traits (burst, bend, first step) has premium value. The Bills also need edge talent: they ranked 19th in sacks over the last two years. An LB prospect with pass rush ability projects directly into a starting OLB/edge role.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: The Carmichael/Payton offensive system is known for featuring RBs heavily in the passing game through screens, checkdowns, and designed routes. James Cook had only 33 receptions for 291 yards in 2025 despite leading the NFL in rushing — a clear underutilization that former Saints QB Chase Daniel has publicly noted will change under Brady/Carmichael. The Payton offense historically made RBs like Alvin Kamara, Darren Sproles, and Reggie Bush into elite receiving threats. Brady's spread concepts with trips formations and empty sets also create natural RB receiving opportunities. Any RB with receiving ability has significantly elevated value here.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Jim Leonhard is a former NFL safety who has always built his defenses around versatile safety play. At Wisconsin, safeties were consistently his unit's strength. His MOFC coverage philosophy requires safeties who can play deep single-high, rotate into the box, cover slot receivers, and trigger downhill against the run. His simulated pressure packages also occasionally involve safeties in blitz roles. Cole Bishop is already being groomed as a centerpiece. Any safety prospect with hybrid versatility — box play, man coverage, blitz ability — has premium value in this defense.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Buffalo's 2026 offseason represents a rare dual-scheme overhaul: the offense is doubling down on Sean Payton-tree principles (more play-action, RB screens, TE receiving emphasis) while the defense is undergoing a wholesale transition from a 4-3 to a 3-4 odd front with simulated pressures. EDGE/OLB is the single highest-value need — Leonhard's 3-4 creates starting OLB roles for pass rushers who can stand up, and the Bills ranked just 19th in sacks the past two years. LB prospects with elite pass rush traits (bend, burst, power) should be valued at EDGE prices for BUF. On offense, WR is a glaring need (Buffalo ranked 29th in yards per catch at 11.6 in 2025), meaning any pass-catcher — including TEs with WR-caliber route running — has inflated value. The Payton-tree RB screen game also means dual-threat RBs get a clear boost. On defense, versatile safeties who can operate in Leonhard's MOFC scheme with box/slot/deep flexibility are premium targets. DT/NT value is moderate — Leonhard needs a true nose tackle for his odd front, but the Bills have Ed Oliver. Slot CB value decreases as the Bills may not deploy a traditional nickel cornerback under Leonhard's 3-4 base with four LBs.
